title: Bioelectrical and bioimpedance measurement using shared electrodes
abstract: A system for bioelectrical signal measurements and bioelectrical impedance sensing includes an oscillator to generate a higher-frequency alternating electrical signal when coupled with a voltage source. It has a first and second electrode to deliver the electrical signal from the oscillator into the skin of a human and to receive intertwined lower-frequency signals with a frequency of up to 400 Hz generated by an electrophysiological source ( e.g. , a muscle) in the human. Electronic circuits process these signals to decouple them based on frequency. A method using this system involves placing electrodes on human skin, generating and delivering a higher-frequency AC electrical signal, receiving and processing the higher-frequency electrical frequency and a lower-frequency bioelectrical signal to decouple the signals, to estimate impedance, and to calculate electrophysiological activity in the human.
claims: Claims CLAIMS What is claimed is: 1. A system for bioelectrical signal measurements and bioelectrical impedance sensing, the system comprising: an oscillator configured to generate a higher-frequency alternating electrical signal when electrically coupled with a voltage source; a first electrode and a second electrode in electrical communication with the oscillator and configured to deliver the electrical signal from the oscillator into a human when the first and second electrodes are in contact with the human and to receive both the higher-frequency electrical signal generated by the oscillator after passing through the human and intertwined lower-frequency electrical signals with a frequency of up to 400 Hz that are generated by an electrophysiological source in the human; and electronic circuits in electrical communication with the first and second electrodes and configured to process the intertwined electrical signals received by the electrodes to decouple (a) the higher-frequency alternating electrical signal generated by the oscillator from (b) the received lower-frequency electrical signals with a frequency of up to 400 Hz from the electrophysiological source, wherein the frequency of the higher-frequency alternating electrical signal is at least 2.5 times the frequency of the received lower-frequency electrical signals from the electrophysiological source. 2. The system of claim 1, wherein the electronic circuits include analog circuits combined with a digital signal processor for measuring impedance. 3. The system of claim 1, wherein the oscillator is configured to generate an alternating electrical signal with a frequency of 1-10 kHz. 4. The system of claim 3, wherein the oscillator is configured to generate an alternating electrical signal with a frequency of 2-8 kHz. 5. The system of claim 3, wherein the lower-frequency electrical signals generated by the electrophysiological source have a frequency of at least 30 Hz. 6. The system of claim 1, wherein the oscillator is a Wien bridge oscillator. 7. The system of claim 1, wherein the electronic circuits include a unity gain inverting stage for single-supply operation. 8. The system of claim 1, wherein the electronic circuits include an amplifier. 9. The system of claim 8, wherein the amplifier is an AC non-inverting gain amplifier. 10. The system of claim 1, further comprising a physiological protection capacitor in electrical communication with at least one of the electrodes and configured to protect a human in contact with the electrodes from receiving an excess voltage discharge from the system. 11. A method for measuring bioelectrical signals using the system of claim 1, the method comprising: placing the first electrode and the second electrode in contact with human skin; using the oscillator to generate a higher-frequency alternating electrical signal; delivering the higher-frequency alternating electrical signal through the first and second electrodes; receiving: a) the higher-frequency oscillator-generated electrical signal and b) lower-frequency bioelectrical signals generated by an electrophysiological source in the human at the first and second electrodes while in contact with the human skin, wherein both the higher-frequency oscillator-generated signals and the lower- frequency bioelectrical signals are received at the first and second electrodes in the same locations, and wherein the frequency of the higher-frequency alternating electrical signal is at least 2.5 times the frequency of the received lower-frequency electrical signals from the electrophysiological source; using the electronic circuits to process (a) the received higher-frequency electrical signals received from the oscillator after passing through the human skin and (b) the received lower-frequency bioelectrical signals to decouple the oscillator-generated electrical signals from the bioelectrical signals generated by the electrophysiological source in the human; estimating electrode-skin interface impedance from the voltage drop in the received decoupled higher-frequency electrical signals in comparison with the higher-frequency electrical signals originally generated by the oscillator; and calculating electrophysiological activity based on the decoupled lower- frequency bioelectrical signals and the estimated electrode-skin interface impedance. 12. The method of claim 11, further comprising amplifying the received higher- frequency electrical signals and lower-frequency bioelectrical signals. 13. The method of claim 11, further comprising protecting the human from a fault in the system using a physiological protection capacitor. 14. The method of claim 11, further comprising measuring force generated by the electrophysiological source when the lower-frequency bioelectrical signals are received. 15. The method of claim 11, wherein the electrode-skin interface impedance is estimated using a digital signal processor. 16. The method of claim 11, further comprising passing the oscillator-generated higher-frequency electrical signal through a unity gain inverting stage before passing into the human skin. 17. The method of claim 11, wherein the method is used for grip force tracking, hand gestures, loaded knee extension tracking, unloaded wrist flexion tracking, or bicep contraction. 18. The method of claim 11, wherein the oscillator generates a higher-frequency alternating electrical signal with a frequency in a range from 1-10 kHz. 19. The method of claim 18, wherein the oscillator generates a higher-frequency alternating electrical signal with a frequency in a range from 2-8 kHz. 20. The method of claim 19, wherein the higher-frequency alternating electrical signal has a frequency of about 5 kHz, the method further comprising using digital band-pass filtering at 4.7-5 kHz to extract the impedance signal and reject unwanted EMG noise. 21. The method of claim 18, wherein the lower-frequency electrical signals generated by the electrophysiological source have a frequency of at least 30 Hz. 22. The method of claim 11, further comprising using a root-mean-square (RMS) envelope with a 500-ms moving window to process the lower-frequency bioelectrical signals and calculate a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). 23. The method of claim 11, wherein the first and second electrodes are placed in contact with the human skin at positions on the other side of the human skin from the electrophysiological source. 24. The method of claim 23, wherein the electrophysiological source is a muscle. 25. The method of claim 24, further comprising placing a third reference or ground electrode over a bony prominence in the human. 26. The method of claim 23, wherein the electrophysiological source is a brain. 27. The method of claim 23, wherein the electrophysiological source is a heart. 28. The method of claim 23, wherein (a) the higher-frequency oscillator-generated electrical signal and (b) the lower-frequency bioelectrical signals generated by the electrophysiological source are simultaneously received at the first and second electrodes while the first and second electrodes remain in contact with the human skin at the positions on the other side from the electrophysiological source. 29. The method of claim 11, wherein the first and second electrodes receive electrophysiological signals from a human organ selected from a gastrointestinal tract, a bladder, an eye, or a peripheral nerve. 30. The method of claim 11, wherein, after the decoupling of the signals, the electrode-skin interface impedance is estimated from the voltage drop in the received higher-frequency signals simultaneously with the calculation of the electrophysiological activity based on the lower-frequency bioelectrical signals.
